episode_title: Jeff Jarvis (Part One): Should AI have the ‘right to read’ news like humans do?
transcript_file: 2024-01-23 - Jeff Jarvis (Part One) Should AI have the ‘right to read’ news like humans do.txt
summary_claims: AI training on news content is fair use because it is transformative — the model does not reproduce content, it learns statistical patterns to comprehend language
Newspapers were not included in copyright until 1909 in the US; the original 1790 US copyright only covered maps, books, and charts
The US Postal Act of 1792 enabled newspapers to send each other copies of their papers for free, intentionally creating a network for news sharing
Newspapers employed 'scissors editors' whose job was to cut content from other newspapers and reprint it — they wanted credit but it was free

guests.name: Jeff Jarvis
guests.role: Director of the Tow Knight Center for Entrepreneurial Journalism; Professor
guests.organization: Craig Newmark Graduate School of Journalism at the City University of New York (CUNY)

matches.theme_name: The Automation Paradox: What AI Replaces, What Remains Irreplaceable

best_quote.text: The biggest threat that AI brings to our business is that it commodifies content. Now comes a machine that can make no end of content. And making content ain't so special. Writing isn't so special. Reporting is. Trust is, authority is, service is. That's where the value of journalism should be resident.
best_quote.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

matches.summary: Jarvis draws a sharp line between commodifiable content production (now automatable) and the irreplaceable human journalistic functions of reporting, trust-building, and service. The episode frames AI not as a replacement for journalists but as a force that exposes where true journalistic value resides.
matches.matched_text: the biggest threat that AI brings to our business is that it commodifies content. Now comes a machine that can make no end of content. And making content ain't so special. Writing isn't so special. Reporting is. Trust is, authority is, service is. That's where the value of journalism should be resident, not in this idea that we make this thing called content that you have to pay for, whether you're a reader or a machine.

matches.theme_name: Trust on the Line: Credibility, Transparency, and the Audience Contract

best_quote.text: One of my students said, whoa, there's a lot of people who never trusted news. And I think that's absolutely true. And we've got to consider that.
best_quote.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

matches.summary: Trust is briefly addressed through Jarvis's observation that the trust crisis predates AI and that many communities never trusted news in the first place, but this is not a central focus of the episode.
matches.matched_text: one of my students who said, whoa, there's a lot of people who never trusted news. And I think that's absolutely true. And we've got to consider that.

matches.theme_name: The Ethics Infrastructure: From Principles on Paper to Practice in the Newsroom

best_quote.text: All it's playing back to us is our cliches and our biases and our misapprehensions about the world as it is.
best_quote.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

matches.summary: Jarvis raises profound ethical concerns about LLMs reflecting the 'publishing of the privileged' and embedding Western biases. The discussion of code-switching for marginalized communities using LLMs, and the need to intentionally compensate for gaps in training data, surfaces structural bias concerns.
matches.matched_text: All it's playing back to us. is our cliches and our biases and our misapprehensions about the world as it is.

matches.theme_name: Building the Machine: Technical Architecture and Engineering Decisions

best_quote.text: They went through a lot of hoops to get the machine to repeat something. They gave them lots of paragraphs of an article, and kind of the only words left for it to predict were those that were left... that was a parlor trick on a parlor trick.
best_quote.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

matches.summary: Technical issues surface through the discussion of LLM memorization as a flaw, the hallucination problem, the currency limitation of models trained to a cutoff date, and the degradation of models trained on AI-generated content ('textpocalypse').
matches.matched_text: They went through a lot of hoops to get the machine to repeat something. They were they gave them lots of paragraphs of an article, and kind of the only words left for it to predict were those that were left. Or from from a Guy Fieri interview where a particularly nasty paragraph was quoted all over media.

matches.theme_name: The Literacy Imperative: Upskilling the Newsroom for an AI Era

best_quote.text: I've argued that LLMs might extend literacy to the extent that they enable people who are intimidated by writing or illustrating to write or illustrate their own stories.
best_quote.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

matches.summary: Jarvis raises the provocative idea of LLMs extending literacy to those intimidated by writing, but this is countered by students who note it could force code-switching to dominant language norms. Amy Reinhardt's CUNY project on a news LLM reflects educational engagement.
matches.matched_text: I've argued that LLMs might extend literacy to the extent that they enable people who are intimidated by writing or illustrating to write or illustrate their own stories.

matches.theme_name: Organizing for AI: Leadership, Strategy, and Institutional Transformation

best_quote.text: News organizations are being quite short-sighted, as they have been in the past... when radio came along newspapers tried to keep them out of the news business.
best_quote.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

matches.summary: The episode frames news industry responses to AI within a historical pattern of protectionist resistance to new technologies (radio, TV, internet), arguing this defensive organizational posture is counterproductive. Jarvis advocates for constructive engagement over litigation.
matches.matched_text: news organizations are being quite short-sighted, as they have been in the past. It's also worth mentioning that when radio came along Newspapers tried to keep them out of the news business.

matches.theme_name: Power, Money, and the Platform: Big Tech and the News Industry

best_quote.text: Does the machine have the same right to read and learn and use information as a journalist? Because every journalist on Earth reads other journalists' work and gets information from sources and does not pay them, learns from it and uses it and repurposes it in their journalism.
best_quote.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

matches.summary: This is the episode's dominant theme. The entire conversation centers on the NYT v. OpenAI lawsuit, fair use in AI training, content licensing versus scraping, the proposed news API, OpenAI's deals with Springer and AP, and the fundamental power struggle over who controls and profits from news content in the AI ecosystem.
matches.matched_text: Does the machine have the same right to read and learn and use information as a journalist? Because every journalist on Earth reads other journalists' work. and gets information from sources and does not pay them, learns from it and uses it and repurposes it in their journalism.

matches.theme_name: Reimagining the News Product: Personalization, Formats, and the Experience Layer

best_quote.text: If it is just data, then you can't own information. You can only own the treatment of it. I argue that news stories are just another form of data visualization. You could make a chart, you could make a box, you could make a story.
best_quote.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

matches.summary: The reframing of news stories as 'data visualization' implicitly opens the door to new product formats, but this is a philosophical point rather than a product discussion.
matches.matched_text: If it is just data, then you can't own information. You can only own the treatment of it, right? And so I think that that's one way to look at this. I mean, I argue that that news stories are just another form of data visualization.

matches.theme_name: Survival Economics: Business Models, Revenue, and Market Position in the AI Era

best_quote.text: Rather than fighting at this point, as we are out of our field, we should be looking for ways to make it possible for AI companies to query and serve information from us and license for that purpose.
best_quote.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

matches.summary: The business model implications of AI are a central concern: the commodification of content, the proposed news API as a revenue mechanism, the distinction between training (fair use) and querying (licensable), and the argument that news organizations must find value beyond content production to survive.
matches.matched_text: Rather than fighting at this point, as we are out of our out of our field. We should, I think, be looking for ways to make it possible for AI companies to query And serve information from us and licensed for that purpose.

matches.theme_name: Regulating the Revolution: Law, Policy, and Journalism as Accountability

best_quote.text: If we regulate and write laws for what we expected [generative AI] to do today, we would completely miss this path of technological development.
best_quote.speaker: Nikita Roy (citing Rebecca Tushnet)

matches.summary: Copyright law, fair use doctrine, and regulatory frameworks are extensively debated. Jarvis provides historical copyright context (1710 Statute of Anne through 1909), discusses Section 230 liability, C-18 in Canada, the Berne Convention, and argues for labor/unfair competition law over copyright as the proper regulatory tool. The liability question (model vs. application vs. user) is explored.
matches.matched_text: This is Newsroom Robots, the podcast where we explore the intersection of artificial intelligence and the news industry. I'm Nikita Roy, data scientist, media entrepreneur, and one of the many founders currently building their. Ventures at the Harvard Innovation Labs. On the Newsroom Robots, I'm excited to bring you insightful conversations with industry experts about how AI is impacting the way we do journalism. Joining me today is Jeff Jarvis. He's been the director of the Tau Knight Center for Entrepreneurial Journalism and a professor at the Craig Newmark Graduate School of Journalism at the City University of New York. He's authored six books, with his latest being The Gutenberg Parenthesis, The Age of Print and Its Lessons for the Age of the Internet. Jeff also co-hosts the podcast This Week in Google and AI Inside.

matches.theme_name: The Prototype Pipeline: From Experiment to Shipped Product

best_quote.text: He sees creating a company around this. And it's starting to do that. And so I think that there are opportunities for us to just think more positively about this rather than going to Congress and courts.
best_quote.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

matches.summary: The proposed news API and a friend's startup venture represent early-stage ideation about productizing the news-AI relationship, but prototyping and production processes are not discussed.
matches.matched_text: he sees creating a company around this. And it's starting to do that. And so I think that there are opportunities for us to just think more positively about this rather than going to Congress and courts.

matches.theme_name: Data Privacy, Source Protection, and the Boundaries of Responsible Use

best_quote.text: Just think about all of the countries that are still not as digitized... what if we go and invest heavily on making, transforming things from print to digital, that's there.
best_quote.speaker: Nikita Roy

matches.summary: Data provenance and quality are substantively discussed: the ethical issues around Books3 acquisition, the problem of models trained on AI-generated content degrading, the Western bias in available training data, and the need to digitize underrepresented regions' content.
matches.matched_text: Just think about all of the countries that are still not as digitized One of the things we had spoken about was like in the African continent. A lot of the governments over there, everything that is out there it's and is digital is actually written from a Western perspective.
